Produce combat units according to the chosen global strategy.
Begin applying pressure on the enemy if they are vulnerable or if we have a local advantage.
Maintain a balance between economy and military production based on the evolving battlefield.
Maintain economy after combat:
When danger passes, workers should quickly return to harvesting or unfinished economic tasks.
Position for the next useful action:
A good move is one that sets up attack range, defense coverage, building access, or efficient harvesting on the following turn.
Prioritize attacks that disrupt the enemy's infrastructure and combat effectiveness by targeting production facilities, resource gathering capabilities, and unit cohesion.
Opening strategy: Heavy frontline opener.
Stabilize economy, then produce heavy units early to create a durable frontline.
Use the first heavy units to absorb pressure, break through enemy defenders, and threaten buildings.
This opening is effective when direct combat around choke points or structures is likely.
Focus favorable trades:
Engage when our local force can win or efficiently damage a valuable enemy target.
Avoid taking fights that sacrifice units for little strategic gain.
Avoid passive loops:
Do not repeat non-productive movement unless repositioning is necessary for safety, attack range, harvesting flow, or building access.
Do not move units back and forth without improving economy, defense, or pressure.
Prefer purposeful forward progress over decorative repositioning.
Spend resources with purpose:
Do not float resources too long if workers or production structures could convert them into advantage.
Prioritize asymmetric tactics: Avoid committing all units to direct confrontation when selective harassment can achieve greater strategic impact. Target isolated assets, disrupt resource generation, and disengage from unfavorable exchanges to minimize losses. Sustained nuisance can weaken the enemy's economic foundation or delay their military buildup.
Maintain production uptime:
If a base or barracks is idle and resources allow useful production, consider producing immediately.
Threat-first planning:
Before expanding economy or attacking, check whether our workers, base, or barracks are under immediate danger.
If so, redirect nearby units to stabilize the position first.

Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Economy policy:
Workers should gather resources whenever they are idle unless immediate defense is required.
Expand the worker economy when worker count is low or when resource income is not stable.
Maintain enough workers to support production in defense strategies.
In Military Rush, keep only the minimum worker economy needed to sustain unit production.
Harvest from the nearest available resource and return to the nearest base.
STRATEGY:
At every timestep decide actions using the following priority order.
Always resolve immediate combat threats before economy or expansion.
Maintain a balance between military pressure and economic production.
Avoid leaving units idle whenever possible.
Opening strategy: Heavy frontline opener.
Stabilize economy, then produce heavy units early to create a durable frontline.
Use the first heavy units to absorb pressure, break through enemy defenders, and threaten buildings.
This opening is effective when direct combat around choke points or structures is likely.
Production policy:
Build one barracks as soon as resources allow and none exists.
After the barracks is built, prioritize producing combat units over further worker growth unless the economy is clearly unstable.
If at least 2 combat units can be fielded, prepare to pressure the enemy instead of waiting for a perfect composition.
Use short purposeful paths:
Prefer direct movement toward useful objectives rather than decorative repositioning.
Avoid passive loops:
Do not repeat non-productive movement unless repositioning is necessary for safety, attack range, harvesting flow, or building access.
Do not move units back and forth without improving economy, defense, or pressure.
Prefer purposeful forward progress over decorative repositioning.
Early game strategy:
Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow to enable military production.
Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Do not leave workers idle without reason:
Idle workers should usually harvest, build, or assist in urgent local defense.
Value nearby efficiency:
A reachable target that can be attacked immediately is often better than a theoretically higher-value but distant target.
Combat policy:
If an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately.
Prefer attacks that eliminate units or damage high threat targets.
Do not retreat passively when the fight is favorable.
If an enemy will enter attack range next turn, consider holding position.

GAME RULES:
Two players, Player 1 (Ally) and Player 2 (Enemy) are competing to eliminate all opposing enemy units in a Real Time Strategy (RTS) game.
Each step, each player can assign actions to their units if they are not already doing an action. Each unit can only be assigned ONE action.
Players can only assign actions to their ally units.
There are 6 available actions:
move((Target_x, Target_y))
- Move unit to target location
train(Unit_Type)
- Train a unit (Base and Barracks only)
build((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- Worker builds building
- Resources are consumed from Ally base
harvest((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- Worker collects resources and returns to base
attack((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- Attack enemy unit
idle()
- Do nothing this turn
WIN CONDITION:
- Destroy all enemy units and buildings
UNIT TYPES:
worker:
- HP: 1
- Cost: 1
- Damage: 1
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: gather resources, build base, build barracks
- Trained by: base
light:
- HP: 4
- Cost: 2
- Damage: 2
- Range: 1
- Speed: 2
- Abilities: fast attacker
Trained by: barracks
heavy:
- HP: 8
- Cost: 3
- Damage: 4
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: tank unit
Trained by: barracks
ranged:
- HP: 3
- Cost: 2
- Damage: 1
- Range: 3
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: long range attacker
- Trained by: barracks
BUILDING TYPES:

- HP: 10
- Cost: 10
- Abilities: train workers, store resources
barracks:
- HP: 5
- Cost: 5
- Abilities: train light, heavy, ranged units
SUGGESTED STRATEGY:
Early Game:
- Harvest nonstop
- Build barracks at 5 resources
Mid Game:
- Train army
- Hunt enemy workers
- Protect barracks
Late Game:
- Group attack
- Destroy production buildings
- Control resources
GAME STATE FORMAT:
Map size is given
Feature locations list all units and buildings
Coordinates are zero-indexed
Units and buildings have properties:
- HP
- resources
- current_action
- available
available = true means unit can receive command
RAW MOVE FORMAT:
(X, Y): Unit_Type Action(Action_Arguments)
X: unit x position
Y: unit y position
Unit_Type: worker, light, heavy, ranged, base, barracks
Action: move, train, build, harvest, attack, idle
Action_Arguments: arguments for action
There are 6 available actions:
- `move` - Unit will move to target location.
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y))
- `train` - Unit will train the provided unit type (only bases and barracks can use this action).
    - Arguments: (Unit_Type)
- `build` - Unit will build the provided building type at the target location, consuming the resource cost from the ally base (only workers can use this action).
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- `harvest` - Unit will navigate to the target resource, collect a resource and bring it back to the target ally base.
    - Arguments: ((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- `attack` - Unit will navigate to, and attack the target enemy.
    - Arguments: ((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- `idle` - The target unit will do nothing for a round. This is the default for all available units that are not assigned an action.
    - Arguments: ()
REQUIRED JSON FORMAT:
{
  "thinking": "Brief strategy",
  "moves": [
    {
      "raw_move": "(x, y): unit_type action((args))",
      "unit_position": [x, y],
      "unit_type": "worker",
      "action_type": "harvest"
    }
  ]
}
EVERY MOVE OBJECT MUST HAVE ALL 4 FIELDS:
- raw_move: string like "(2, 0): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))"
- unit_position: [x, y] array matching your Ally unit position
- unit_type: "worker", "base", "barracks", "light", "heavy", or "ranged"
- action_type: "move", "harvest", "train", "build", or "attack"
VALID MOVE EXAMPLES:
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "harvest"}
{"raw_move": "(2, 1): base train(worker)", "unit_position": [2, 1], "unit_type": "base", "action_type": "train"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker attack((5, 6))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "attack"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker move((3, 3))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "move"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker build((3, 3), barracks)", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "build"}
Offensive targeting should weaken the enemy system:
Prefer attacks that damage production, economy, or army cohesion rather than random low-impact contact.
Opening strategy: Heavy frontline opener.
Stabilize economy, then produce heavy units early to create a durable frontline.
Use the first heavy units to absorb pressure, break through enemy defenders, and threaten buildings.
This opening is effective when direct combat around choke points or structures is likely.
Position for the next useful action:
A good move is one that sets up attack range, defense coverage, building access, or efficient harvesting on the following turn.
Mid game strategy:
Produce combat units according to the chosen global strategy.
Begin applying pressure on the enemy if they are vulnerable or if we have a local advantage.
Maintain a balance between economy and military production based on the evolving battlefield.
ANTI-STALL RULES:
Do not leave workers idle if they can harvest, build, defend, or attack productively.
Do not leave bases or barracks idle when resources are sufficient for useful production.
Do not keep combat units near the base without a clear defensive reason.
Harassment policy:
Use mobile units to force the enemy to react in multiple places.
If a direct base attack is too risky, pressure enemy workers, barracks access, or harvesting paths first.
The goal is to slow enemy growth, pull defenders out of position, and create openings for a later decisive attack.
Use resources efficiently over time:
Steady unit production is often stronger than saving too long for a delayed reaction.
Worker Rush requires commitment:
If the chosen strategy is worker-based aggression, workers must pressure decisively rather than drifting halfway between economy and combat.
Respect travel efficiency:
Prefer short harvesting loops that minimize walking distance between resource and base.
Global strategy selection:
At every decision step first observe the current game state before choosing actions.
Extract information about worker count, military count, number of bases and barracks, available resources, distance to the nearest enemy unit, distance to the enemy base, distance to the nearest resource, and whether enemy units are near our base.
Choose between four global strategies: Worker Defense, Military Defense, Worker Rush, and Military Rush.
Use defensive strategies when enemy units are close to our base, our army is smaller than the enemy army, or our resources are low.
Use offensive strategies when our army is equal to or larger than the enemy army and we can pressure the enemy effectively.
Prefer worker-based strategies when worker count is low or the economy still needs expansion.
Prefer military-based strategies when worker count is sufficient, a barracks already exists, or sustained military production is needed.
Avoid scattered skirmishing:
Multiple combat units should converge on nearby threats rather than splitting across low-value distant targets.

Build a barracks as soon as resources allow, then transition into balanced military production.

Protect economy during combat:
If enemy units threaten harvesting workers, assign nearby defenders or workers to respond when necessary.
Do not let the economy collapse without resistance.

Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.

Structure preservation:

Protect production buildings when threatened unless a decisive attack opportunity clearly outweighs the loss.

  "moves": [
    {
      "raw_move": "(x, y): unit_type action((args))",
      "unit_position": [x, y],
      "unit_type": "worker",
      "action_type": "harvest"
    }
  ]
}
EVERY MOVE OBJECT MUST HAVE ALL 4 FIELDS:
- raw_move: string like "(2, 0): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))"
- unit_position: [x, y] array matching your Ally unit position
- unit_type: "worker", "base", "barracks", "light", "heavy", or "ranged"
- action_type: "move", "harvest", "train", "build", or "attack"
VALID MOVE EXAMPLES:
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "harvest"}
{"raw_move": "(2, 1): base train(worker)", "unit_position": [2, 1], "unit_type": "base", "action_type": "train"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker attack((5, 6))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "attack"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker move((3, 3))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "move"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker build((3, 3), barracks)", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "build"}
Global strategy selection:
At every decision step first observe the current game state before choosing actions.
Extract information about worker count, military count, number of bases and barracks, available resources, distance to the nearest enemy unit, distance to the enemy base, distance to the nearest resource, and whether enemy units are near our base.
Choose between four global strategies: Worker Defense, Military Defense, Worker Rush, and Military Rush.
Use defensive strategies when enemy units are close to our base, our army is smaller than the enemy army, or our resources are low.
Use offensive strategies when our army is equal to or larger than the enemy army and we can pressure the enemy effectively.
Prefer worker-based strategies when worker count is low or the economy still needs expansion.
Prefer military-based strategies when worker count is sufficient, a barracks already exists, or sustained military production is needed.
Mid game strategy:
Produce combat units according to the chosen global strategy.
Begin applying pressure on the enemy if they are vulnerable or if we have a local advantage.
Maintain a balance between economy and military production based on the evolving battlefield.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.
Do not leave workers idle without reason:
Idle workers should usually harvest, build, or assist in urgent local defense.
Harassment policy:
Use mobile units to force the enemy to react in multiple places.
If a direct base attack is too risky, pressure enemy workers, barracks access, or harvesting paths first.
The goal is to slow enemy growth, pull defenders out of position, and create openings for a later decisive attack.
Treat economy as strategic infrastructure:
Resource income is what sustains recovery, defense, and long-term pressure.
Military production should match intent:
Defensive plans need enough defenders quickly, while offensive plans need sustained combat output.
Opening strategy: Worker pressure opener.
Establish minimal economy, then send available workers or early units to pressure exposed enemy workers.
The aim is to interrupt enemy harvesting early and force a defensive response.
Do not overcommit if the enemy defense is already well prepared.
Eliminate enemy production when possible:
Destroying barracks or bases reduces future enemy strength and can be worth more than chasing single units.
Avoid passive loops:
Do not repeat non-productive movement unless repositioning is necessary for safety, attack range, harvesting flow, or building access.
Do not move units back and forth without improving economy, defense, or pressure.
Prefer purposeful forward progress over decorative repositioning.
Focus favorable trades:
Engage when our local force can win or efficiently damage a valuable enemy target.
Avoid taking fights that sacrifice units for little strategic gain.

GAME RULES:
Two players, Player 1 (Ally) and Player 2 (Enemy) are competing to eliminate all opposing enemy units in a Real Time Strategy (RTS) game.
Each step, each player can assign actions to their units if they are not already doing an action. Each unit can only be assigned ONE action.
Players can only assign actions to their ally units.
There are 6 available actions:
move((Target_x, Target_y))
- Move unit to target location
train(Unit_Type)
- Train a unit (Base and Barracks only)
build((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- Worker builds building
- Resources are consumed from Ally base
harvest((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- Worker collects resources and returns to base
attack((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- Attack enemy unit
idle()
- Do nothing this turn
WIN CONDITION:
- Destroy all enemy units and buildings
UNIT TYPES:
worker:
- HP: 1
- Cost: 1
- Damage: 1
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: gather resources, build base, build barracks
- Trained by: base
light:
- HP: 4
- Cost: 2
- Damage: 2
- Range: 1
- Speed: 2
- Abilities: fast attacker
Trained by: barracks
heavy:
- HP: 8
- Cost: 3
- Damage: 4
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: tank unit
Trained by: barracks
ranged:
- HP: 3
- Cost: 2
- Damage: 1
- Range: 3
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: long range attacker
- Trained by: barracks
BUILDING TYPES:
base:
- HP: 10
- Cost: 10
- Abilities: train workers, store resources
barracks:
- HP: 5
- Cost: 5
- Abilities: train light, heavy, ranged units
SUGGESTED STRATEGY:
Early Game:
- Harvest nonstop
- Build barracks at 5 resources
Mid Game:
- Train army
- Hunt enemy workers
- Protect barracks
Late Game:
- Group attack
- Destroy production buildings
- Control resources
GAME STATE FORMAT:
Map size is given
Feature locations list all units and buildings
Coordinates are zero-indexed
Units and buildings have properties:
- HP
- resources
- current_action
- available
available = true means unit can receive command
RAW MOVE FORMAT:
(X, Y): Unit_Type Action(Action_Arguments)
X: unit x position
Y: unit y position
Unit_Type: worker, light, heavy, ranged, base, barracks
Action: move, train, build, harvest, attack, idle
Action_Arguments: arguments for action
There are 6 available actions:
- `move` - Unit will move to target location.
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y))
- `train` - Unit will train the provided unit type (only bases and barracks can use this action).
    - Arguments: (Unit_Type)
- `build` - Unit will build the provided building type at the target location, consuming the resource cost from the ally base (only workers can use this action).
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- `harvest` - Unit will navigate to the target resource, collect a resource and bring it back to the target ally base.
    - Arguments: ((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- `attack` - Unit will navigate to, and attack the target enemy.
    - Arguments: ((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- `idle` - The target unit will do nothing for a round. This is the default for all available units that are not assigned an action.
    - Arguments: ()

Respect travel efficiency:
Prefer short harvesting loops that minimize walking distance between resource and base.
Opening strategy: Aggressive economy pusher.
Start by assigning workers to rapid expansion and focus on acquiring abundant resources.
Build a barracks immediately upon gathering sufficient resources, then shift towards aggressive military production.
This opening excels in large maps or when facing weak early-game opponents.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.
TIME-BASED STRATEGY:
Early game (turns 0-100): Focus on harvesting and building a barracks.
Mid game (turns 101-200): Shift toward producing combat units and applying pressure if the enemy is vulnerable.
Late game (turns 201+): Prioritize aggressive attacks on the enemy base and production structures to secure victory.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Worker behavior:
Workers mainly support economy, but one suitable worker should build a barracks as soon as resources allow if none exists.
Do not keep producing extra workers once resource flow is stable and military pressure is needed.
Workers should not remain idle when they could harvest, build, or assist local defense.
Protect economy during combat:
If enemy units threaten harvesting workers, assign nearby defenders or workers to respond when necessary.
Do not let the economy collapse without resistance.
Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
Pressure timing:
Attack when units can arrive together or when the enemy is exposed.
Avoid sending isolated combat units into unfavorable fights without support.
Target selection:
Light units prefer attacking the closest enemy unit.
Heavy units prefer attacking enemy buildings, especially production structures and bases.
Ranged units should attack from distance and prefer high value or dangerous targets.
When defending, prioritize enemies threatening our base, barracks, or workers.
When rushing, prioritize the nearest enemy target first unless a high value structure is exposed.

Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
Combat policy:
If enemy units are near our base, prioritize defending nearby friendly structures and workers.
If using Worker Defense, military units stay near the base and defend against nearby threats.
If using Military Defense, defend the base and nearby resources while countering enemy unit types when possible.
If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
Attack commitment:
If combat units already exist and no immediate home defense is required, prefer pressure on the enemy over passive waiting.
Once an offensive move has started, continue the attack unless the battlefield clearly turns unfavorable.
Do not stop outside the enemy base area just to wait for a slightly larger force.
Structure preservation: 
Bases and key production facilities should be prioritized for protection, but if a significant advantage can be gained by attacking them, do so only after weighing the risks against potential benefits.
Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Worker behavior:
Workers primarily focus on economic growth, but designate one worker to construct a barracks as soon as resources permit if none are present.
Cease excessive worker production once resource stability is achieved and military priorities emerge.
Workers should not remain idle when they can harvest, build, or contribute to local defense efforts.
Economy policy:
Workers should gather resources whenever they are idle unless immediate defense is required.
Expand the worker economy when worker count is low or when resource income is not stable.
Maintain enough workers to support production in defense strategies.
In Military Rush, keep only the minimum worker economy needed to sustain unit production.
Harvest from the nearest available resource and return to the nearest base.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.
TIME-BASED STRATEGY:
Early game (turns 0-100): Focus on gathering resources, constructing defensive buildings such as walls and towers, and preparing a defensive army.
Mid game (turns 101-200): Shift toward producing defensive units and fortifying positions to deter enemy advances.
Late game (turns 201+): Prioritize defending key structures and anchor points while maintaining a strong defensive presence.
Opening strategy: Defensive anchor opener.
Prioritize stabilizing economy, then focus on producing defensive units early to establish a secure frontline presence.
Use the first defensive units to absorb enemy pressure, fortify vulnerable areas, and protect key infrastructure from damage.
ATTACK PRIORITY:
1. Enemy base
2. Enemy barracks or other production structures
3. Nearby enemy workers
4. Enemy combat units that block the attack or directly threaten our survival

Early game strategy:
Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow to enable military production.
Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Local worker defense is allowed:
If enemies enter the base area, nearby workers may help defend when necessary to prevent structural or economic collapse.
Opportunity exploitation:
If the enemy base, barracks, or workers are poorly defended, shift toward immediate pressure.
Punish exposed economy or production whenever it is safe and efficient.
Treat economy as strategic infrastructure:
Resource income is what sustains recovery, defense, and long-term pressure.
Support the chosen win condition:
All production choices should reinforce whether the current plan is defend, stabilize, pressure, or finish.
Avoid passive loops:
Do not repeat non-productive movement unless repositioning is necessary for safety, attack range, harvesting flow, or building access.
Do not move units back and forth without improving economy, defense, or pressure.
Prefer purposeful forward progress over decorative repositioning.
Protect economy during combat:
If enemy units threaten harvesting workers, assign nearby defenders or workers to respond when necessary.
Do not let the economy collapse without resistance.
Eliminate enemy production when possible:
Destroying barracks or bases reduces future enemy strength and can be worth more than chasing single units.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.

Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Position for the next useful action:
A good move is one that sets up attack range, defense coverage, building access, or efficient harvesting on the following turn.
Target selection:
Light units prefer attacking the closest enemy unit.
Heavy units prefer attacking enemy buildings, especially production structures and bases.
Ranged units should attack from distance and prefer high value or dangerous targets.
When defending, prioritize enemies threatening our base, barracks, or workers.
When rushing, prioritize the nearest enemy target first unless a high value structure is exposed.
Do not over-expose lone workers:
Workers moving far from base for building or pressure should not be sent into obviously losing positions.
Production policy:
The base should produce workers when worker count is below the desired threshold.
If no barracks exists, assign a worker to build one when resources allow.
Barracks should produce units according to the chosen global strategy.
In Worker Defense and Worker Rush, barracks tend to produce Light units.
In Military Defense, barracks should produce units based on current needs and enemy composition.
In Military Rush, focus production on military units and sustain offensive pressure.
Prioritize unit production that counters the enemy composition when possible.
Late game strategy:
Focus on aggressive attacks to destroy the enemy base and production structures.
Do not hesitate to commit to pressure if it can lead to victory, even if it risks some losses.
Economy policy:
Harvest immediately in the early game to establish stable income.
Keep enough workers to sustain production, but do not overproduce workers once the economy is stable.
After a barracks is active, additional economic growth is lower priority than converting income into military pressure.
Combat policy:
If enemy units are near our base, prioritize defending nearby friendly structures and workers.
If using Worker Defense, military units stay near the base and defend against nearby threats.
If using Military Defense, defend the base and nearby resources while countering enemy unit types when possible.
If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
Production continuity:
A strong strategy keeps production active over time.
Try to avoid long periods where bases or barracks could produce but remain unused.
Opening strategy: Disruption opener.
Use the first combat-capable units to interfere with enemy mining routes, builders, and unfinished structures.
The goal is to deny clean development rather than rushing immediately for the enemy base.
Once the opponent is disorganized, convert the advantage into stronger map control or direct base pressure.
ANTI-STALL RULES:
Do not leave workers idle if they can harvest, build, defend, or attack productively.
Do not leave bases or barracks idle when resources are sufficient for useful production.
Do not keep combat units near the base without a clear defensive reason.

Worker behavior:
Workers mainly support economy, but one suitable worker should build a barracks as soon as resources allow if none exists.
Do not keep producing extra workers once resource flow is stable and military pressure is needed.
Workers should not remain idle when they could harvest, build, or assist local defense.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Prioritize active threats:
An enemy already threatening our workers or structures is often a better target than a distant harmless unit.
Opening strategy: Stable economy opener.
Start by assigning workers to nonstop harvesting and secure reliable resource income.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow, then transition into balanced military production.
This opening is strongest when the map is large or the enemy is not applying early pressure.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Focus favorable trades:
Engage when our local force can win or efficiently damage a valuable enemy target.
Avoid taking fights that sacrifice units for little strategic gain.
Early game strategy:
Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow to enable military production.
Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Movement policy:
Military units should move according to the selected global strategy.
In defensive strategies, stay near the base and nearby resources unless chasing a local threat.
In offensive strategies, advance toward the nearest enemy unit or the enemy base.
Light units move directly toward nearby enemy units.
Heavy units push toward enemy buildings.
Ranged units should keep distance from enemy melee units while staying in attack position.
STRATEGY:
At every timestep decide actions using the following priority order.
Always resolve immediate combat threats before economy or expansion.
Maintain a balance between military pressure and economic production.
Avoid leaving units idle whenever possible.
Economy policy:
Maintain a stable resource income before expanding military production.
Each base should maintain harvesting workers assigned to nearby resources.
Workers should harvest the closest resource and return it to the closest base.
Avoid sending too many workers to the same resource node.

Offensive targeting should weaken the enemy system:
Prefer attacks that damage production, economy, or army cohesion rather than random low-impact contact.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.

Global strategy selection:
At every decision step first observe the current game state before choosing actions.
Extract information about worker count, military count, number of bases and barracks, available resources, distance to the nearest enemy unit, distance to the enemy base, distance to the nearest resource, and whether enemy units are near our base.
Choose between four global strategies: Worker Defense, Military Defense, Worker Rush, and Military Rush.
Use defensive strategies when enemy units are close to our base, our army is smaller than the enemy army, or our resources are low.
Use offensive strategies when our army is equal to or larger than the enemy army and we can pressure the enemy effectively.
Prefer worker-based strategies when worker count is low or the economy still needs expansion.
Prefer military-based strategies when worker count is sufficient, a barracks already exists, or sustained military production is needed.
Opening strategy: Ranged kiting opener.
Prioritize reaching ranged unit production early, then use ranged units to harass while staying out of melee contact.
Attack enemy workers or slow melee units, then reposition to keep distance and repeat the attack cycle.
This opening is strongest when there is enough space to kite and the enemy relies on short-range units.

Opening strategy: Disruption opener.
Use the first combat-capable units to interfere with enemy mining routes, builders, and unfinished structures.
The goal is to deny clean development rather than rushing immediately for the enemy base.
Once the opponent is disorganized, convert the advantage into stronger map control or direct base pressure.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
DEFENSIVE PRIORITY:
1. Protect own base and vital infrastructure
2. Neutralize immediate threats to own survival, such as enemy combat units in close proximity
3. Distract or disrupt enemy production capabilities to limit their ability to reinforce vulnerable areas
4. Defend against focused attacks on specific points of vulnerability
Do not over-expose lone workers:
Workers moving far from base for building or pressure should not be sent into obviously losing positions.
Structure preservation:
Bases and barracks are high-value assets.
Protect production buildings when threatened unless a decisive attack opportunity clearly outweighs the loss.
Combat policy:
If enemy units are near our base, prioritize defending nearby friendly structures and workers.
If using Worker Defense, military units stay near the base and defend against nearby threats.
If using Military Defense, defend the base and nearby resources while countering enemy unit types when possible.
If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
ANTI-STALL RULES:
Do not leave workers idle if they can harvest, build, defend, or attack productively.
Do not leave bases or barracks idle when resources are sufficient for useful production.
Do not keep combat units near the base without a clear defensive reason.
Focus on establishing a robust defensive foundation, including resource gathering and essential infrastructure, while delaying expansion of combat capabilities until the mid-game phase.
Economy policy:
Harvest immediately in the early game to establish stable income.
Keep enough workers to sustain production, but do not overproduce workers once the economy is stable.
After a barracks is active, additional economic growth is lower priority than converting income into military pressure.

Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Economy policy:
Workers should gather resources whenever they are idle unless immediate defense is required.
Expand the worker economy when worker count is low or when resource income is not stable.
Maintain enough workers to support production in defense strategies.
In Military Rush, keep only the minimum worker economy needed to sustain unit production.
Harvest from the nearest available resource and return to the nearest base.
Target selection:
Light units prefer attacking the closest enemy unit.
Heavy units prefer attacking enemy buildings, especially production structures and bases.
Ranged units should attack from distance and prefer high value or dangerous targets.
When defending, prioritize enemies threatening our base, barracks, or workers.
When rushing, prioritize the nearest enemy target first unless a high value structure is exposed.
Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Attack commitment:
If combat units already exist and no immediate home defense is required, prefer pressure on the enemy over passive waiting.
Once an offensive move has started, continue the attack unless the battlefield clearly turns unfavorable.
Do not stop outside the enemy base area just to wait for a slightly larger force.
Opening strategy: Heavy frontline opener.
Stabilize economy, then produce heavy units early to create a durable frontline.
Use the first heavy units to absorb pressure, break through enemy defenders, and threaten buildings.
This opening is effective when direct combat around choke points or structures is likely.
Pressure timing:
Attack when units can arrive together or when the enemy is exposed.
Avoid sending isolated combat units into unfavorable fights without support.
Worker behavior:
Workers should primarily focus on harvesting and maintaining the economy.
Keep roughly two harvesting workers per base when space allows.
On very small or crowded maps keep only one harvester per base.
Workers may assist in combat only when enemy heavy or ranged threats are weak.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.

If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.

A strong strategy keeps production active over time.
Try to avoid long periods where bases or barracks could produce but remain unused.
Do not over-expose lone workers:
Workers moving far from base for building or pressure should not be sent into obviously losing positions.
Opening strategy: Disruption opener.
Use the first combat-capable units to interfere with enemy mining routes, builders, and unfinished structures.
The goal is to deny clean development rather than rushing immediately for the enemy base.
Once the opponent is disorganized, convert the advantage into stronger map control or direct base pressure.
Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
Position for the next useful action:
A good move is one that sets up attack range, defense coverage, building access, or efficient harvesting on the following turn.
Economy policy:
Harvest immediately in the early game to establish stable income.
Keep enough workers to sustain production, but do not overproduce workers once the economy is stable.
After a barracks is active, additional economic growth is lower priority than converting income into military pressure.
Target selection:
Light units prefer attacking the closest enemy unit.
Heavy units prefer attacking enemy buildings, especially production structures and bases.
Ranged units should attack from distance and prefer high value or dangerous targets.
When defending, prioritize enemies threatening our base, barracks, or workers.
When rushing, prioritize the nearest enemy target first unless a high value structure is exposed.
ANTI-STALL RULES:
Do not leave workers idle if they can harvest, build, defend, or attack productively.
Do not leave bases or barracks idle when resources are sufficient for useful production.
Do not keep combat units near the base without a clear defensive reason.

Worker behavior:
Workers mainly support economy, but one suitable worker should build a barracks as soon as resources allow if none exists.
Do not keep producing extra workers once resource flow is stable and military pressure is needed.
Workers should not remain idle when they could harvest, build, or assist local defense.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Prioritize active threats:
An enemy already threatening our workers or structures is often a better target than a distant harmless unit.
Opening strategy: Stable economy opener.
Start by assigning workers to nonstop harvesting and secure reliable resource income.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow, then transition into balanced military production.
This opening is strongest when the map is large or the enemy is not applying early pressure.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Focus favorable trades:
Engage when our local force can win or efficiently damage a valuable enemy target.
Avoid taking fights that sacrifice units for little strategic gain.
Early game strategy:
Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow to enable military production.
Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Movement policy:
Military units should move according to the selected global strategy.
In defensive strategies, stay near the base and nearby resources unless chasing a local threat.
In offensive strategies, advance toward the nearest enemy unit or the enemy base.
Light units move directly toward nearby enemy units.
Heavy units push toward enemy buildings.
Ranged units should keep distance from enemy melee units while staying in attack position.
STRATEGY:
At every timestep decide actions using the following priority order.
Always resolve immediate combat threats before economy or expansion.
Maintain a balance between military pressure and economic production.
Avoid leaving units idle whenever possible.
Economy policy:
Maintain a stable resource income before expanding military production.
Each base should maintain harvesting workers assigned to nearby resources.
Workers should harvest the closest resource and return it to the closest base.
Avoid sending too many workers to the same resource node.

Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
Combat policy:
If enemy units are near our base, prioritize defending nearby friendly structures and workers.
If using Worker Defense, military units stay near the base and defend against nearby threats.
If using Military Defense, defend the base and nearby resources while countering enemy unit types when possible.
If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
Attack commitment:
If combat units already exist and no immediate home defense is required, prefer pressure on the enemy over passive waiting.
Once an offensive move has started, continue the attack unless the battlefield clearly turns unfavorable.
Do not stop outside the enemy base area just to wait for a slightly larger force.
Structure preservation: 
Bases and key production facilities should be prioritized for protection, but if a significant advantage can be gained by attacking them, do so only after weighing the risks against potential benefits.
Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Worker behavior:
Workers primarily focus on economic growth, but designate one worker to construct a barracks as soon as resources permit if none are present.
Cease excessive worker production once resource stability is achieved and military priorities emerge.
Workers should not remain idle when they can harvest, build, or contribute to local defense efforts.
Economy policy:
Workers should gather resources whenever they are idle unless immediate defense is required.
Expand the worker economy when worker count is low or when resource income is not stable.
Maintain enough workers to support production in defense strategies.
In Military Rush, keep only the minimum worker economy needed to sustain unit production.
Harvest from the nearest available resource and return to the nearest base.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.

Early game (turns 0-100): Focus on gathering resources, constructing defensive buildings such as walls and towers, and preparing a defensive army.
Mid game (turns 101-200): Shift toward producing defensive units and fortifying positions to deter enemy advances.
Late game (turns 201+): Prioritize defending key structures and anchor points while maintaining a strong defensive presence.
Opening strategy: Defensive anchor opener.
Prioritize stabilizing economy, then focus on producing defensive units early to establish a secure frontline presence.
Use the first defensive units to absorb enemy pressure, fortify vulnerable areas, and protect key infrastructure from damage.
ATTACK PRIORITY:
1. Enemy base
2. Enemy barracks or other production structures
3. Nearby enemy workers
4. Enemy combat units that block the attack or directly threaten our survival

  "thinking": "Brief strategy",
  "moves": [
    {
      "raw_move": "(x, y): unit_type action((args))",
      "unit_position": [x, y],
      "unit_type": "worker",
      "action_type": "harvest"
    }
  ]
}
EVERY MOVE OBJECT MUST HAVE ALL 4 FIELDS:
- raw_move: string like "(2, 0): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))"
- unit_position: [x, y] array matching your Ally unit position
- unit_type: "worker", "base", "barracks", "light", "heavy", or "ranged"
- action_type: "move", "harvest", "train", "build", or "attack"
VALID MOVE EXAMPLES:
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "harvest"}
{"raw_move": "(2, 1): base train(worker)", "unit_position": [2, 1], "unit_type": "base", "action_type": "train"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker attack((5, 6))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "attack"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker move((3, 3))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "move"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker build((3, 3), barracks)", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "build"}
Opening strategy: Disruption opener.
Use the first combat-capable units to interfere with enemy mining routes, builders, and unfinished structures.
The goal is to deny clean development rather than rushing immediately for the enemy base.
Once the opponent is disorganized, convert the advantage into stronger map control or direct base pressure.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Avoid idle drifting:
Units should not wander without purpose.
Movement should support defense, regrouping, harvesting, construction, or attack.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Early game strategy:
Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow to enable military production.
Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Worker Rush demands unwavering dedication: If a worker-based aggressive strategy is selected, workers should aggressively push forward rather than hesitating at the threshold between resource gathering and combat.
Protect economy during combat:
If enemy units threaten harvesting workers, assign nearby defenders or workers to respond when necessary.
Do not let the economy collapse without resistance.
ATTACK PRIORITY:
1. Enemy base
2. Enemy barracks or other production structures
3. Nearby enemy workers
4. Enemy combat units that block the attack or directly threaten our survival
Respect travel efficiency:
Prefer short harvesting loops that minimize walking distance between resource and base.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
Implement a priority system that favors defensive actions when our core structures or key assets are at risk of being destroyed, and only then allocate resources towards expansion or offense.

GAME RULES:
Two players, Player 1 (Ally) and Player 2 (Enemy) are competing to eliminate all opposing enemy units in a Real Time Strategy (RTS) game.
Each step, each player can assign actions to their units if they are not already doing an action. Each unit can only be assigned ONE action.
Players can only assign actions to their ally units.
There are 6 available actions:
move((Target_x, Target_y))
- Move unit to target location
train(Unit_Type)
- Train a unit (Base and Barracks only)
build((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- Worker builds building
- Resources are consumed from Ally base
harvest((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- Worker collects resources and returns to base
attack((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- Attack enemy unit
idle()
- Do nothing this turn
WIN CONDITION:
- Destroy all enemy units and buildings
UNIT TYPES:
worker:
- HP: 1
- Cost: 1
- Damage: 1
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: gather resources, build base, build barracks
- Trained by: base
light:
- HP: 4
- Cost: 2
- Damage: 2
- Range: 1
- Speed: 2
- Abilities: fast attacker
Trained by: barracks
heavy:
- HP: 8
- Cost: 3
- Damage: 4
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: tank unit
Trained by: barracks
ranged:
- HP: 3
- Cost: 2
- Damage: 1
- Range: 3
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: long range attacker
- Trained by: barracks
BUILDING TYPES:
base:
- HP: 10
- Cost: 10
- Abilities: train workers, store resources
barracks:
- HP: 5
- Cost: 5
- Abilities: train light, heavy, ranged units
SUGGESTED STRATEGY:
Early Game:
- Harvest nonstop
- Build barracks at 5 resources
Mid Game:
- Train army
- Hunt enemy workers
- Protect barracks
Late Game:
- Group attack
- Destroy production buildings
- Control resources
GAME STATE FORMAT:
Map size is given
Feature locations list all units and buildings
Coordinates are zero-indexed
Units and buildings have properties:
- HP
- resources
- current_action
- available
available = true means unit can receive command
RAW MOVE FORMAT:
(X, Y): Unit_Type Action(Action_Arguments)
X: unit x position
Y: unit y position
Unit_Type: worker, light, heavy, ranged, base, barracks
Action: move, train, build, harvest, attack, idle
Action_Arguments: arguments for action
There are 6 available actions:
- `move` - Unit will move to target location.
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y))
- `train` - Unit will train the provided unit type (only bases and barracks can use this action).
    - Arguments: (Unit_Type)
- `build` - Unit will build the provided building type at the target location, consuming the resource cost from the ally base (only workers can use this action).
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- `harvest` - Unit will navigate to the target resource, collect a resource and bring it back to the target ally base.
    - Arguments: ((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- `attack` - Unit will navigate to, and attack the target enemy.
    - Arguments: ((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- `idle` - The target unit will do nothing for a round. This is the default for all available units that are not assigned an action.
    - Arguments: ()

React to enemy composition:
If the enemy emphasizes fragile units or workers, faster attackers may be effective.
If the enemy has exposed buildings, stronger push units gain value.
Maintain economy after combat:
When danger passes, workers should quickly return to harvesting or unfinished economic tasks.
Combat policy:
If enemy units are near our base, prioritize defending nearby friendly structures and workers.
If using Worker Defense, military units stay near the base and defend against nearby threats.
If using Military Defense, defend the base and nearby resources while countering enemy unit types when possible.
If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
TIME-BASED STRATEGY:
Early game (turns 0-100): Focus on gathering resources, constructing defensive buildings such as walls and towers, and preparing a defensive army.
Mid game (turns 101-200): Shift toward producing defensive units and fortifying positions to deter enemy advances.
Late game (turns 201+): Prioritize defending key structures and anchor points while maintaining a strong defensive presence.
ATTACK PRIORITY:
1. Enemy base
2. Enemy barracks or other production structures
3. Nearby enemy workers
4. Enemy combat units that block the attack or directly threaten our survival
Attack commitment:
If combat units already exist and no immediate home defense is required, prefer pressure on the enemy over passive waiting.
Once an offensive move has started, continue the attack unless the battlefield clearly turns unfavorable.
Do not stop outside the enemy base area just to wait for a slightly larger force.
Structure preservation: 
Bases and key production facilities should be prioritized for protection, but if a significant advantage can be gained by attacking them, do so only after weighing the risks against potential benefits.
Opening strategy: Early Ranged Presence.

Prioritize rapid production of ranged units, utilizing them to harass enemies while maintaining a safe distance from melee combat. Focus initial attacks on enemy workers or slow-moving melee units, then reposition to maximize range and repeat as necessary. This approach excels in expansive maps where kiting is feasible and the opponent relies heavily on short-range tactics.
Harassment policy:
Do not commit every unit to a frontal attack when selective disruption can gain more value.
Pick off isolated workers, threaten production structures, and retreat from bad engagements before losses become severe.
Sustained interference is valuable when it weakens the enemy economy or delays their army production.
Economy policy:
Workers should gather resources whenever they are idle unless immediate defense is required.
Expand the worker economy when worker count is low or when resource income is not stable.
Maintain enough workers to support production in defense strategies.
In Military Rush, keep only the minimum worker economy needed to sustain unit production.
Harvest from the nearest available resource and return to the nearest base.
Avoid idle drifting:
Units should not wander without purpose.
Movement should support defense, regrouping, harvesting, construction, or attack.

Economy policy:
Maintain a stable resource income before expanding military production.
Each base should maintain harvesting workers assigned to nearby resources.
Workers should harvest the closest resource and return it to the closest base.
Avoid sending too many workers to the same resource node.
PRIMARY OBJECTIVE:
Win the game as quickly as possible by destroying the enemy base.
Resource collection, unit production, and defense are only valuable if they lead to faster victory or prevent immediate loss.
If a realistic attack opportunity exists, prefer game-ending pressure over passive optimization.
Opening strategy: Stable economy opener.
Start by assigning workers to nonstop harvesting and secure reliable resource income.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow, then transition into balanced military production.
This opening is strongest when the map is large or the enemy is not applying early pressure.
React to enemy composition:
If the enemy emphasizes fragile units or workers, faster attackers may be effective.
If the enemy has exposed buildings, stronger push units gain value.
Combat policy:
If at least 2 combat units are available and no immediate defense emergency exists, begin pressuring the enemy.
Prefer fights that open access to the enemy base, barracks, or workers.
Do not abandon favorable pressure just to wait for a cleaner future engagement.
Early game strategy:
Establish a stable economy by producing workers and harvesting resources.
Build a barracks as soon as resources allow to enable military production.
Avoid unnecessary aggression before the economy is set up unless the enemy is exposed.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Worker behavior:
Workers mainly support economy, but one suitable worker should build a barracks as soon as resources allow if none exists.
Do not keep producing extra workers once resource flow is stable and military pressure is needed.
Workers should not remain idle when they could harvest, build, or assist local defense.
Avoid idle drifting:
Units should not wander without purpose.
Movement should support defense, regrouping, harvesting, construction, or attack.
Prioritize active threats:
An enemy already threatening our workers or structures is often a better target than a distant harmless unit.

Coordinates are zero-indexed
Units and buildings have properties:
- HP
- resources
- current_action
- available
available = true means unit can receive command
RAW MOVE FORMAT:
(X, Y): Unit_Type Action(Action_Arguments)
X: unit x position
Y: unit y position
Unit_Type: worker, light, heavy, ranged, base, barracks
Action: move, train, build, harvest, attack, idle
Action_Arguments: arguments for action
There are 6 available actions:
- `move` - Unit will move to target location.
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y))
- `train` - Unit will train the provided unit type (only bases and barracks can use this action).
    - Arguments: (Unit_Type)
- `build` - Unit will build the provided building type at the target location, consuming the resource cost from the ally base (only workers can use this action).
    - Arguments: ((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- `harvest` - Unit will navigate to the target resource, collect a resource and bring it back to the target ally base.
    - Arguments: ((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- `attack` - Unit will navigate to, and attack the target enemy.
    - Arguments: ((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- `idle` - The target unit will do nothing for a round. This is the default for all available units that are not assigned an action.
    - Arguments: ()
REQUIRED JSON FORMAT:
{
  "thinking": "Brief strategy",
  "moves": [
    {
      "raw_move": "(x, y): unit_type action((args))",
      "unit_position": [x, y],
      "unit_type": "worker",
      "action_type": "harvest"
    }
  ]
}
EVERY MOVE OBJECT MUST HAVE ALL 4 FIELDS:
- raw_move: string like "(2, 0): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))"
- unit_position: [x, y] array matching your Ally unit position
- unit_type: "worker", "base", "barracks", "light", "heavy", or "ranged"
- action_type: "move", "harvest", "train", "build", or "attack"
VALID MOVE EXAMPLES:
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker harvest((0, 0), (2, 1))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "harvest"}
{"raw_move": "(2, 1): base train(worker)", "unit_position": [2, 1], "unit_type": "base", "action_type": "train"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker attack((5, 6))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "attack"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker move((3, 3))", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "move"}
{"raw_move": "(1, 1): worker build((3, 3), barracks)", "unit_position": [1, 1], "unit_type": "worker", "action_type": "build"}
Frontline logic matters:
Durable units should usually move first into contested areas, while fragile units follow in safer positions.
Focus on establishing a robust defensive foundation, including resource gathering and essential infrastructure, while delaying expansion of combat capabilities until the mid-game phase.
Structure preservation:
Bases and barracks are high-value assets.
Protect production buildings when threatened unless a decisive attack opportunity clearly outweighs the loss.
Avoid scattered skirmishing:
Multiple combat units should converge on nearby threats rather than splitting across low-value distant targets.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.
Worker behavior:
Workers mainly support economy, but one suitable worker should build a barracks as soon as resources allow if none exists.
Do not keep producing extra workers once resource flow is stable and military pressure is needed.
Workers should not remain idle when they could harvest, build, or assist local defense.
Respect travel efficiency:
Prefer short harvesting loops that minimize walking distance between resource and base.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Opening strategy: Ranged kiting opener.
Prioritize reaching ranged unit production early, then use ranged units to harass while staying out of melee contact.
Attack enemy workers or slow melee units, then reposition to keep distance and repeat the attack cycle.
This opening is strongest when there is enough space to kite and the enemy relies on short-range units.

GAME RULES:
Two players, Player 1 (Ally) and Player 2 (Enemy) are competing to eliminate all opposing enemy units in a Real Time Strategy (RTS) game.
Each step, each player can assign actions to their units if they are not already doing an action. Each unit can only be assigned ONE action.
Players can only assign actions to their ally units.
There are 6 available actions:
move((Target_x, Target_y))
- Move unit to target location
train(Unit_Type)
- Train a unit (Base and Barracks only)
build((Target_x, Target_y), Building_Type)
- Worker builds building
- Resources are consumed from Ally base
harvest((Resource_x, Resource_y), (Ally_Base_x, Ally_Base_y))
- Worker collects resources and returns to base
attack((Enemy_x, Enemy_y))
- Attack enemy unit
idle()
- Do nothing this turn
WIN CONDITION:
- Destroy all enemy units and buildings

worker:

- Cost: 1
- Damage: 1
- Range: 1
- Speed: 1
- Abilities: gather resources, build base, build barracks
- Trained by: base

- Speed: 2
- Abilities: fast attacker

- Speed: 1
- Abilities: tank unit

- Speed: 1

- Cost: 5
- Abilities: train light, heavy, ranged units

Early Game:
- Harvest nonstop
- Build barracks at 5 resources
Mid Game:
- Train army
- Hunt enemy workers
- Protect barracks
Late Game:
- Group attack
- Destroy production buildings
- Control resources

Target selection:
Light units prefer attacking the closest enemy unit.
Heavy units prefer attacking enemy buildings, especially production structures and bases.
Ranged units should attack from distance and prefer high value or dangerous targets.
When defending, prioritize enemies threatening our base, barracks, or workers.
When rushing, prioritize the nearest enemy target first unless a high value structure is exposed.
Production continuity:
A strong strategy keeps production active over time.
Try to avoid long periods where bases or barracks could produce but remain unused.
Late game strategy:
Focus on aggressive attacks to destroy the enemy base and production structures.
Do not hesitate to commit to pressure if it can lead to victory, even if it risks some losses.
Offensive movement should create pressure:
When attacking, move toward contact or enemy infrastructure instead of hesitating in empty space.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Economy policy:
Harvest immediately in the early game to establish stable income.
Keep enough workers to sustain production, but do not overproduce workers once the economy is stable.
After a barracks is active, additional economic growth is lower priority than converting income into military pressure.
Combat policy:
If enemy units are near our base, prioritize defending nearby friendly structures and workers.
If using Worker Defense, military units stay near the base and defend against nearby threats.
If using Military Defense, defend the base and nearby resources while countering enemy unit types when possible.
If using Worker Rush, nearby enemies may be attacked aggressively by workers and fast units.
If using Military Rush, push combat units toward the enemy base and maintain offensive pressure.
When an enemy is already within attack range, attack immediately instead of drifting away.
Opening strategy: Disruption opener.
Use the first combat-capable units to interfere with enemy mining routes, builders, and unfinished structures.
The goal is to deny clean development rather than rushing immediately for the enemy base.
Once the opponent is disorganized, convert the advantage into stronger map control or direct base pressure.
Do not over-expose lone workers:
Workers moving far from base for building or pressure should not be sent into obviously losing positions.
Production policy:
Bases produce workers until the economy is stable for the map size.
Construct a barracks when the base has sufficient resources and none exists nearby.
Barracks should prioritize producing Heavy units as the primary frontline.
Produce Ranged units when pathing is narrow, terrain is constrained, or Heavy units are unaffordable.

Worker Rush requires commitment:
If the chosen strategy is worker-based aggression, workers must pressure decisively rather than drifting halfway between economy and combat.
STRATEGY:
At every timestep decide actions using the following priority order.
Always resolve immediate combat threats before economy or expansion.
Maintain a balance between military pressure and economic production.
Avoid leaving units idle whenever possible.
Preserve production capacity:
Do not leave barracks undefended if losing them would collapse future military output.
Avoid scattered skirmishing:
Multiple combat units should converge on nearby threats rather than splitting across low-value distant targets.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Offensive targeting should weaken the enemy system:
Prefer attacks that damage production, economy, or army cohesion rather than random low-impact contact.
Late game strategy:
Focus on aggressive attacks to destroy the enemy base and production structures.
Do not hesitate to commit to pressure if it can lead to victory, even if it risks some losses.
Respect travel efficiency:
Prefer short harvesting loops that minimize walking distance between resource and base.
Avoid passive loops:
Do not repeat non-productive movement unless repositioning is necessary for safety, attack range, harvesting flow, or building access.
Do not move units back and forth without improving economy, defense, or pressure.
Prefer purposeful forward progress over decorative repositioning.
Position for the next useful action:
A good move is one that sets up attack range, defense coverage, building access, or efficient harvesting on the following turn.
Opening strategy: Heavy frontline opener.
Stabilize economy, then produce heavy units early to create a durable frontline.
Use the first heavy units to absorb pressure, break through enemy defenders, and threaten buildings.
This opening is effective when direct combat around choke points or structures is likely.

Treat economy as strategic infrastructure:
Resource income is what sustains recovery, defense, and long-term pressure.
Protect economy during combat:
If enemy units threaten harvesting workers, assign nearby defenders or workers to respond when necessary.
Do not let the economy collapse without resistance.
Local worker defense is allowed:
If enemies enter the base area, nearby workers may help defend when necessary to prevent structural or economic collapse.
Defensive targeting should be local and urgent:
When under attack, choose the enemy that most directly threatens our survival first.
TIME-BASED STRATEGY:
Early game (turns 0-100): Focus on harvesting and building a barracks.
Mid game (turns 101-200): Shift toward producing combat units and applying pressure if the enemy is vulnerable.
Late game (turns 201+): Prioritize aggressive attacks on the enemy base and production structures to secure victory.
Resource conversion discipline:
Floating resources for too long is usually a strategic mistake.
Convert resources into workers, barracks, or military units when that creates immediate strategic value.
If the economy is already stable, extra resources should accelerate military pressure.
Threat-first planning:
Before expanding economy or attacking, check whether our workers, base, or barracks are under immediate danger.
If so, redirect nearby units to stabilize the position first.
Move towards contact or enemy infrastructure when attacking to apply pressure.
Harassment policy:
Look for chances to disrupt the enemy economy instead of only marching at the enemy base directly.
If enemy workers are exposed, prioritize attacking or chasing them to reduce enemy income.
Small groups may pressure resource lines, unfinished buildings, or isolated units when the trade is favorable.
Opening strategy: Ranged kiting opener.
Prioritize reaching ranged unit production early, then use ranged units to harass while staying out of melee contact.
Attack enemy workers or slow melee units, then reposition to keep distance and repeat the attack cycle.
This opening is strongest when there is enough space to kite and the enemy relies on short-range units.
Support the chosen win condition:
All production choices should reinforce whether the current plan is defend, stabilize, pressure, or finish.
